phrases.text_local: Bonjour, je voudrais réserver une table pour deux personnes, samedi soir à vingt heures.
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR, zhuh voo-DRAY ray-zair-VAY oon TAH-bluh poor duh pair-SON, sahm-DEE swahr ah van-TURE.
phrases.cultural_note: Dinner reservations in France are taken seriously — showing up without one, especially on weekends, is considered poor planning. 8 PM ('vingt heures') is a typical dinner start time in France.

responses.french: Bien sûr, monsieur. À quel nom, s'il vous plaît ?
responses.english: Of course, sir. Under what name, please?
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR, muh-SYUH. ah kel NOHN, seel voo PLAY ?

responses.french: Je suis désolé, nous sommes complets samedi soir. Dimanche vous conviendrait-il ?
responses.english: I'm sorry, we're fully booked Saturday evening. Would Sunday work for you?
responses.phonetic: zhuh swee day-zo-LAY, noo som kom-PLAY sahm-DEE swahr. dee-MAHNSH voo kohn-VYAN-dray-teel ?

phrases.text_local: Je vais prendre le canard, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh vay PRAHN-druh luh kah-NAR, seel voo PLAY.
phrases.cultural_note: Duck ('le canard') is iconic in French cuisine — from duck confit to magret de canard. Ordering it in a French-inspired restaurant signals that you know your classics.

responses.french: Excellent choix ! Vous le voulez rosé ou bien cuit ?
responses.english: Excellent choice! Would you like it medium or well done?
responses.phonetic: ex-seh-LAHN SHWAH ! voo luh voo-LAY ro-ZAY oo byan KWEE ?

responses.french: Le canard est accompagné d'une purée de céleri-rave ce soir.
responses.english: The duck comes with a celeriac purée this evening.
responses.phonetic: luh kah-NAR ay tah-koh-pah-NYAY doon poo-RAY duh say-luh-REE rav suh SWAHR.

phrases.text_local: Pourriez-vous nous recommander un vin pour accompagner le canard ?
phrases.pronunciation: poor-YAY voo noo ruh-koh-mahn-DAY un VAN poor ah-koh-pah-NYAY luh kah-NAR ?
phrases.cultural_note: Asking for a wine pairing recommendation ('un accord mets-vins') is second nature in France. The server will appreciate the question and likely explain the choice.

responses.french: Je vous conseillerais un Pinot Noir de Bourgogne. Il se marie parfaitement avec le canard.
responses.english: I would recommend a Burgundy Pinot Noir. It pairs perfectly with duck.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo kohn-say-yuh-RAY un pee-NOH NWAHR duh boor-GON-yuh. eel suh mah-REE par-fay-MAHN ah-VEK luh kah-NAR.

phrases.text_local: C'était absolument délicieux. Pourriez-vous transmettre mes compliments au chef ?
phrases.pronunciation: say-TAY ahb-so-loo-MAHN day-lee-SYUH. poor-YAY voo trahn-MET-ruh may kom-plee-MAHN oh SHEF ?
phrases.cultural_note: Sending compliments to the chef ('les compliments au chef') is a deeply appreciated gesture in France — it honors the artistry of cooking, which is taken very seriously.

responses.french: Avec plaisir, merci. Ça lui fera très plaisir.
responses.english: With pleasure, thank you. That will make him very happy.
responses.phonetic: ah-VEK play-ZEER, mair-SEE. sah lwee fuh-RAH tray play-ZEER.

phrases.cultural_note: In France, the bill is never brought until you ask — leaving it on the table unsolicited would be considered rude, as it implies the server wants you to leave. Always ask for 'l'addition.'

responses.french: Je vous l'apporte dans un instant.
responses.english: I'll bring it to you in a moment.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo lah-PORT dahn zun an-STAHN.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the dessert is homemade
phrases.text_local: Est-ce que les desserts sont faits maison ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess-KUH lay day-SAIR sohn fay may-ZOHN ?
phrases.cultural_note: 'Fait maison' (homemade) is a quality label in French restaurants — since 2014, French law requires restaurants to display whether dishes are homemade. It's a legitimate and savvy question to ask.

responses.french: Absolument ! Notre tarte tatin et notre crème brûlée sont entièrement faites maison.
responses.english: Absolutely! Our tarte tatin and crème brûlée are entirely homemade.
responses.phonetic: ahb-so-loo-MAHN ! notr TART tah-TAN ay notr KREM broo-LAY sohn ahn-tyair-MAHN fay may-ZOHN.
